title: Acute Cholecystitis
oneLiner: Inflammation of the gallbladder (often due to gallstone obstruction of cystic duct); common cause of RUQ pain and sepsis in adults, especially women >40.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Myocardial infarction (inferior/right ventricular):** ECG – look for inferior ST elevation, right-sided leads if clinical suspicion; pain may be epigastric/RUQ, not chest.
- **Perforated peptic ulcer:** Board-like rigidity, free gas under diaphragm on erect CXR (or CT); pain may be sudden, diffuse, not localised to RUQ.
- **Acute pancreatitis:** Lipase >3x upper limit of normal – do not wait for abdominal CT; this is the single test to rule out before assuming cholecystitis.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** Pulsatile abdominal mass, hypotension, flank/back pain; bedside ultrasound if AAA suspected – do not miss this.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Constant, severe RUQ or epigastric pain (often post-prandial, especially after fatty meal)
  - Nausea, vomiting, anorexia
  - Fever, chills (if infected/empyema)
  - Referred pain to right shoulder/scapula (phrenic nerve irritation)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - RUQ tenderness ± guarding/rigidity
  - **Murphy's sign:** inspiratory arrest on palpation of RUQ – positive if catch in breath (specific but not sensitive)
  - ± palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign – suggests malignant obstruction, not acute cholecystitis)
  - Fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea if septic
  - Jaundice if common bile duct obstruction/complication
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Biliary colic:** Pain resolves within hours, no fever, no systemic inflammation; normal CRP/WCC, normal US (stone without wall thickening)
- **Acute cholangitis:** Charcot's triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) + sepsis; dilated CBD ± stones on US; needs urgent ERCP
- **Pancreatitis:** Lipase elevated; pain often epigastric with radiation to back; amylase less specific
- **Peptic ulcer disease/gastritis:** Epigastric tenderness; no fever, normal LFTs, no gallstones on US; respond to PPI
- **Hepatitis:** LFTs markedly elevated (ALT/AST >500), negative RUQ US, prodrome of malaise/joint pain
- **Right lower lobe pneumonia / pleurisy:** Cough, respiratory symptoms, chest findings; CXR abnormal
- **Renal colic (right):** Flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no fever; normal RUQ US
- **Appendicitis (retrocaecal):** May present with RUQ pain; but Murphy's sign negative, US shows no gallbladder pathology; CT if diagnostic doubt
- **Splitter:** **Positive Murphy's sign** points strongly to acute cholecystitis vs biliary colic or other causes; if Murphy's negative but pain + fever + abnormal LFTs, consider cholangitis
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (to rule out MI – especially in elderly, diabetic, or atypical presentations)
  - Urine pregnancy test (women of childbearing age)
  - Urinalysis (RPG negative)
  - Bedside ultrasound if available (by trained operator) – look for gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>4mm), pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC:** leucocytosis (WCC >12)
  - **CRP:** often >50
  - **LFTs:** ALP, GGT, bilirubin – may be normal or mildly elevated; if markedly elevated (bilirubin >40, ALP >2x ULN) suspect CBD obstruction
  - **Lipase:** mandatory – to exclude pancreatitis (if lipase >3x ULN, pancreatitis)
  - **Urea + Cr:** baseline for fluid resuscitation
  - **Blood cultures:** if febrile/septic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Right upper quadrant ultrasound – sensitivity >95% for gallstones; look for wall thickening, sludge, pericholecystic fluid
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if US equivocal, or if complications suspected – perforation, abscess, or if alternative diagnosis like pancreatitis/AAA)
  - **If bile duct obstruction suspected:** MRCP (elective) or ERCP (therapeutic if cholangitis)
  - **HIDA scan:** rarely used now; reserved for acalculous cholecystitis if US negative
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Tokyo Guidelines 2018** – requires:
  - Signs of local inflammation (Murphy's, RUQ mass, pain) PLUS
  - Systemic signs (fever, raised CRP, raised WCC) PLUS
  - Imaging findings (wall thickening, gallstones, etc.)
  - Definite if all three; probable if two
- **Never-miss test:** **Lipase** – failure to check and miss pancreatitis is a common and serious error that can lead to prolonged fasting, inappropriate surgery, or missed necrotising pancreatitis
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **NBM** – nil by mouth (if planned for surgery or if severe)
  - **IV fluids:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's or 0.9% saline) – 1L stat, then 125 mL/hr (adjust for hydration status, age, heart failure)
  - **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g IV; Morphine 5-10mg IV titrated (no evidence morphine worsens outcomes – pain control is priority)
  - **IV antibiotics** if signs of infection (fever, WCC>12, CRP>100, or septic): start empirical broad-spectrum (see Drugs section)
  - **Anti-emetics:** Ondansetron 4-8mg IV or Cyclizine 50mg IV
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids until oral diet tolerated (when pain settling, NBM not required if mild)
  - Monitor vitals/NEWS2; watch for deterioration -> sepsis
  - **Liaise with surgical team** regarding timing of cholecystectomy: early (within 48-72h) is preferred if mild-moderate and fit for surgery; delayed (6-12 weeks) if severe or high-risk
  - **ERCP** if cholangitis or jaundice + CBD stone on imaging (stenting +/or stone extraction)
  - **Cholecystostomy** (drainage) if too unstable for surgery (e.g., elderly ICU patients)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy (if delayed approach) – ideally within 6 weeks
  - No role for "gallbladder flushing" or dissolution therapy in acute cases
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** Hartmann's 125 mL/hr (if no heart/renal failure)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if CrCl<30 or weight <50kg)
  - **Diet:** NBM initially then clear fluids → low-fat diet when pain improves; if not NBM, consider NG tube if vomiting/ileus
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (more frequent if abnormal)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line antibiotics (for mild-moderate cholecystitis):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV tds** (or amoxicillin 1g IV tds + metronidazole 500mg IV tds if penicillin allergy)
  - **Alternative (if penicillin allergy or high risk of resistant organisms):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV tds OR ceftriaxone 2g IV od + metronidazole 500mg IV tds
  - **Duration:** 3-5 days (stop if afebrile, WCC down, CRP falling); longer if severe/complicated
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g IV/PO qds (max 4g/day)
  - Morphine 5-10mg IV as required (max 20mg in 4 hours)
  - NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac 50mg PO tds) – avoid if renal impairment, gastrointestinal bleeding, or >75 years
- **Anti-emetics:**
  - Ondansetron 4-8mg IV/PO tds (max 24mg/day; caution – QT prolongation with other QT-prolonging drugs)
  - Cyclizine 50mg IV/PO tds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in penicillin allergy, hepatic impairment
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid if hyperbilirubinaemia (neonates); can cause biliary sludging (but still used in adults)
  - Metronidazole: avoid in first trimester (pregnancy) if possible; potentiates warfarin
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if CrCl<30 (check local policy)
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce dose if CrCl<20
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment needed in renal impairment except severe
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** overdosing in renal failure (risk of seizures). Always check eGFR and adjust if CrCl<30.
  - **Gentamicin** (if used for severe sepsis/empyema): check levels before second dose; high trough increases nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity. UK practice prefers alternative regimens.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Empyema of gallbladder:** Pus-filled gallbladder; suspect if high fever, rigors, persistent pain despite antibiotics; US shows sludge/echogenic material; needs drainage (cholecystostomy) or urgent cholecystectomy
  - **Gallbladder perforation:** Sudden worsening pain, peritonitis; CT shows free fluid/air; emergency surgery
  - **Cholangitis** (ascending infection of bile duct) – see Differential
  - **Gallstone pancreatitis** – lipase >3x ULN; manage supportively, ERCP if severe with cholangitis
  - **Gallstone ileus** – small bowel obstruction from large gallstone eroding into duodenum; rare but high mortality in elderly
  - **Cholecystenteric fistula** – gallstone erodes into bowel; may cause bilious vomiting, pneumobilia
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent cholecystitis (if not operated)
  - Chronic cholecystitis (wall thickening, fibrosis) – may be found incidentally
  - Porcelain gallbladder (calcification) – risk of gallbladder carcinoma
sections.Prognosis: - **Uncomplicated acute cholecystitis:** Mortality <1% if early cholecystectomy in fit patients. More severe if elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised. Gallstone pancreatitis carries ~5-10% mortality. Delayed treatment increases risk of empyema/perforation.
sections.Pearls (Positive Actions):
- **Always check lipase** before assuming cholecystitis – saves missing pancreatitis and its critical management.
- **Perform bedside Murphy's sign** – if positive, sensitivity >50% but specificity >80%; negative Murphy's makes cholecystitis less likely.
- **If jaundice or dilated CBD on US, call surgical reg now** – need MRCP/ERCP before cholecystectomy to clear duct stones; don't wait.
- **In elderly/diabetic patients** – sepsis can develop rapidly; start antibiotics early and escalate to senior regime if NEWS2 >5.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don't start antibiotics before blood cultures** in septic patients – you lose the chance to target therapy.
- **Don't schedule elective cholecystectomy without checking for CBD stones** – if LFTs abnormal or US shows dilated duct, need MRCP or ERCP first.
- **Don't continue NBM for days if the patient is not severely ill and pain is controlled** – prolonged starvation causes deconditioning; start clear fluids when pain settles.
- **Don't forget VTE prophylaxis** in all surgical patients (unless contraindicated) – laparoscopic cholecystectomy implies pneumoperitoneum + surgical stress.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening RUQ pain (especially with fever, rigors, vomiting)
  - Development of jaundice (yellow skin/eyes, dark urine, pale stools)
  - Signs of sepsis (feeling feverish, confused, breathless, unable to eat/drink)
  - If discharged with antibiotics, complete full course and attend follow-up US
- **Follow-up:**
  - Surgical outpatient review in 2-4 weeks for scheduled cholecystectomy (if delayed)
  - If no surgery planned (e.g., unfit), repeat US in 6 months to monitor for complications
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call surgical senior / reg:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1L IV fluid
  - High suspicion of cholangitis (jaundice + RUQ pain + fever) – needs ERCP
  - Widespread peritonitis, or suspicion of perforation
  - Failure to improve after 24h of antibiotics and supportive care
- **Call ICU/Anaesthesia:**
  - Septic shock (persistent hypotension, vasopressor support needed, lactate >4)
  - GCS <15 or altered mental state
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2)
  - Acute kidney injury (rising creatinine, oliguria) despite fluid resuscitation

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Cholecystitis
**One-liner:** Inflammation of the gallbladder (often due to gallstone obstruction of cystic duct); common cause of RUQ pain and sepsis in adults, especially women >40.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Myocardial infarction (inferior/right ventricular):** ECG – look for inferior ST elevation, right-sided leads if clinical suspicion; pain may be epigastric/RUQ, not chest.
- **Perforated peptic ulcer:** Board-like rigidity, free gas under diaphragm on erect CXR (or CT); pain may be sudden, diffuse, not localised to RUQ.
- **Acute pancreatitis:** Lipase >3x upper limit of normal – do not wait for abdominal CT; this is the single test to rule out before assuming cholecystitis.
- **Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA):** Pulsatile abdominal mass, hypotension, flank/back pain; bedside ultrasound if AAA suspected – do not miss this.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Constant, severe RUQ or epigastric pain (often post-prandial, especially after fatty meal)
  - Nausea, vomiting, anorexia
  - Fever, chills (if infected/empyema)
  - Referred pain to right shoulder/scapula (phrenic nerve irritation)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - RUQ tenderness ± guarding/rigidity
  - **Murphy's sign:** inspiratory arrest on palpation of RUQ – positive if catch in breath (specific but not sensitive)
  - ± palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier's sign – suggests malignant obstruction, not acute cholecystitis)
  - Fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea if septic
  - Jaundice if common bile duct obstruction/complication

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Biliary colic:** Pain resolves within hours, no fever, no systemic inflammation; normal CRP/WCC, normal US (stone without wall thickening)
- **Acute cholangitis:** Charcot's triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) + sepsis; dilated CBD ± stones on US; needs urgent ERCP
- **Pancreatitis:** Lipase elevated; pain often epigastric with radiation to back; amylase less specific
- **Peptic ulcer disease/gastritis:** Epigastric tenderness; no fever, normal LFTs, no gallstones on US; respond to PPI
- **Hepatitis:** LFTs markedly elevated (ALT/AST >500), negative RUQ US, prodrome of malaise/joint pain
- **Right lower lobe pneumonia / pleurisy:** Cough, respiratory symptoms, chest findings; CXR abnormal
- **Renal colic (right):** Flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no fever; normal RUQ US
- **Appendicitis (retrocaecal):** May present with RUQ pain; but Murphy's sign negative, US shows no gallbladder pathology; CT if diagnostic doubt
- **Splitter:** **Positive Murphy's sign** points strongly to acute cholecystitis vs biliary colic or other causes; if Murphy's negative but pain + fever + abnormal LFTs, consider cholangitis

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (to rule out MI – especially in elderly, diabetic, or atypical presentations)
  - Urine pregnancy test (women of childbearing age)
  - Urinalysis (RPG negative)
  - Bedside ultrasound if available (by trained operator) – look for gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>4mm), pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC:** leucocytosis (WCC >12)
  - **CRP:** often >50
  - **LFTs:** ALP, GGT, bilirubin – may be normal or mildly elevated; if markedly elevated (bilirubin >40, ALP >2x ULN) suspect CBD obstruction
  - **Lipase:** mandatory – to exclude pancreatitis (if lipase >3x ULN, pancreatitis)
  - **Urea + Cr:** baseline for fluid resuscitation
  - **Blood cultures:** if febrile/septic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Right upper quadrant ultrasound – sensitivity >95% for gallstones; look for wall thickening, sludge, pericholecystic fluid
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if US equivocal, or if complications suspected – perforation, abscess, or if alternative diagnosis like pancreatitis/AAA)
  - **If bile duct obstruction suspected:** MRCP (elective) or ERCP (therapeutic if cholangitis)
  - **HIDA scan:** rarely used now; reserved for acalculous cholecystitis if US negative
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Tokyo Guidelines 2018** – requires:
  - Signs of local inflammation (Murphy's, RUQ mass, pain) PLUS
  - Systemic signs (fever, raised CRP, raised WCC) PLUS
  - Imaging findings (wall thickening, gallstones, etc.)
  - Definite if all three; probable if two
- **Never-miss test:** **Lipase** – failure to check and miss pancreatitis is a common and serious error that can lead to prolonged fasting, inappropriate surgery, or missed necrotising pancreatitis

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **NBM** – nil by mouth (if planned for surgery or if severe)
  - **IV fluids:** crystalloid (e.g. Hartmann's or 0.9% saline) – 1L stat, then 125 mL/hr (adjust for hydration status, age, heart failure)
  - **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g IV; Morphine 5-10mg IV titrated (no evidence morphine worsens outcomes – pain control is priority)
  - **IV antibiotics** if signs of infection (fever, WCC>12, CRP>100, or septic): start empirical broad-spectrum (see Drugs section)
  - **Anti-emetics:** Ondansetron 4-8mg IV or Cyclizine 50mg IV
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids until oral diet tolerated (when pain settling, NBM not required if mild)
  - Monitor vitals/NEWS2; watch for deterioration -> sepsis
  - **Liaise with surgical team** regarding timing of cholecystectomy: early (within 48-72h) is preferred if mild-moderate and fit for surgery; delayed (6-12 weeks) if severe or high-risk
  - **ERCP** if cholangitis or jaundice + CBD stone on imaging (stenting +/or stone extraction)
  - **Cholecystostomy** (drainage) if too unstable for surgery (e.g., elderly ICU patients)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy (if delayed approach) – ideally within 6 weeks
  - No role for "gallbladder flushing" or dissolution therapy in acute cases
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** Hartmann's 125 mL/hr (if no heart/renal failure)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if CrCl<30 or weight <50kg)
  - **Diet:** NBM initially then clear fluids → low-fat diet when pain improves; if not NBM, consider NG tube if vomiting/ileus
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 (more frequent if abnormal)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line antibiotics (for mild-moderate cholecystitis):**
  - **Co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV tds** (or amoxicillin 1g IV tds + metronidazole 500mg IV tds if penicillin allergy)
  - **Alternative (if penicillin allergy or high risk of resistant organisms):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV tds OR ceftriaxone 2g IV od + metronidazole 500mg IV tds
  - **Duration:** 3-5 days (stop if afebrile, WCC down, CRP falling); longer if severe/complicated
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g IV/PO qds (max 4g/day)
  - Morphine 5-10mg IV as required (max 20mg in 4 hours)
  - NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac 50mg PO tds) – avoid if renal impairment, gastrointestinal bleeding, or >75 years
- **Anti-emetics:**
  - Ondansetron 4-8mg IV/PO tds (max 24mg/day; caution – QT prolongation with other QT-prolonging drugs)
  - Cyclizine 50mg IV/PO tds
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: caution in penicillin allergy, hepatic impairment
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid if hyperbilirubinaemia (neonates); can cause biliary sludging (but still used in adults)
  - Metronidazole: avoid in first trimester (pregnancy) if possible; potentiates warfarin
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: reduce dose if CrCl<30 (check local policy)
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce dose if CrCl<20
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment needed in renal impairment except severe
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** overdosing in renal failure (risk of seizures). Always check eGFR and adjust if CrCl<30.
  - **Gentamicin** (if used for severe sepsis/empyema): check levels before second dose; high trough increases nephrotoxicity/ototoxicity. UK practice prefers alternative regimens.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Empyema of gallbladder:** Pus-filled gallbladder; suspect if high fever, rigors, persistent pain despite antibiotics; US shows sludge/echogenic material; needs drainage (cholecystostomy) or urgent cholecystectomy
  - **Gallbladder perforation:** Sudden worsening pain, peritonitis; CT shows free fluid/air; emergency surgery
  - **Cholangitis** (ascending infection of bile duct) – see Differential
  - **Gallstone pancreatitis** – lipase >3x ULN; manage supportively, ERCP if severe with cholangitis
  - **Gallstone ileus** – small bowel obstruction from large gallstone eroding into duodenum; rare but high mortality in elderly
  - **Cholecystenteric fistula** – gallstone erodes into bowel; may cause bilious vomiting, pneumobilia
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent cholecystitis (if not operated)
  - Chronic cholecystitis (wall thickening, fibrosis) – may be found incidentally
  - Porcelain gallbladder (calcification) – risk of gallbladder carcinoma

### Prognosis
- **Uncomplicated acute cholecystitis:** Mortality <1% if early cholecystectomy in fit patients. More severe if elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised. Gallstone pancreatitis carries ~5-10% mortality. Delayed treatment increases risk of empyema/perforation.

### Pearls (Positive Actions)
- **Always check lipase** before assuming cholecystitis – saves missing pancreatitis and its critical management.
- **Perform bedside Murphy's sign** – if positive, sensitivity >50% but specificity >80%; negative Murphy's makes cholecystitis less likely.
- **If jaundice or dilated CBD on US, call surgical reg now** – need MRCP/ERCP before cholecystectomy to clear duct stones; don't wait.
- **In elderly/diabetic patients** – sepsis can develop rapidly; start antibiotics early and escalate to senior regime if NEWS2 >5.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don't start antibiotics before blood cultures** in septic patients – you lose the chance to target therapy.
- **Don't schedule elective cholecystectomy without checking for CBD stones** – if LFTs abnormal or US shows dilated duct, need MRCP or ERCP first.
- **Don't continue NBM for days if the patient is not severely ill and pain is controlled** – prolonged starvation causes deconditioning; start clear fluids when pain settles.
- **Don't forget VTE prophylaxis** in all surgical patients (unless contraindicated) – laparoscopic cholecystectomy implies pneumoperitoneum + surgical stress.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening RUQ pain (especially with fever, rigors, vomiting)
  - Development of jaundice (yellow skin/eyes, dark urine, pale stools)
  - Signs of sepsis (feeling feverish, confused, breathless, unable to eat/drink)
  - If discharged with antibiotics, complete full course and attend follow-up US
- **Follow-up:**
  - Surgical outpatient review in 2-4 weeks for scheduled cholecystectomy (if delayed)
  - If no surgery planned (e.g., unfit), repeat US in 6 months to monitor for complications

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call surgical senior / reg:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (or rising)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 1L IV fluid
  - High suspicion of cholangitis (jaundice + RUQ pain + fever) – needs ERCP
  - Widespread peritonitis, or suspicion of perforation
  - Failure to improve after 24h of antibiotics and supportive care
- **Call ICU/Anaesthesia:**
  - Septic shock (persistent hypotension, vasopressor support needed, lactate >4)
  - GCS <15 or altered mental state
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2)
  - Acute kidney injury (rising creatinine, oliguria) despite fluid resuscitation
